1:
"In those days there was no king in Israel: and in those days the tribe of the Danites sought them an inheritance to dwell in; for unto that day all their inheritance had not fallen unto them among the tribes of Israel." The witnesses note that there was no king in Israel, and Dan sought an inheritance not yet fully possessed. The witnesses note that Gill notes there was no supreme magistrate or judge, this repeated to account for the disorders. The witnesses note that all their inheritance had not fallen to them among the tribes. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no king in Israel — the disorders; the Dan sought an inheritance — to dwell in; the not yet possessed — their lot unwon; the disorder without rule — where there is no vision, the people perish (Prov. 29:18).

The witnesses agree the chapter opens with the refrain that explains the disorders of the age — "In those days there was no king in Israel": with no settled rule, every man did as he pleased, and confusion and apostasy spread; Dan, moreover, "sought them an inheritance," not having possessed the lot God gave them, for they had failed to drive out the enemy (Judg. 1:34); the want of godly government and the restlessness of an unpossessed inheritance set the scene for the sad story to follow; it teaches that where there is no rule of God acknowledged, men fall into disorder, and that the failure to possess what God gives breeds wandering and trouble, "where there is no vision, the people perish" (Prov. 29:18), no king in Israel and Dan seeking an inheritance.

So the witnesses make the verse no king in Israel: The witnesses note the no king in Israel: the disorders. The witnesses note the Dan sought an inheritance: to dwell in. The witnesses note the not yet possessed: their lot unwon. The witnesses note the disorder without rule: where there is no vision, the people perish (Prov. 29:18). The expositors observe the no king in Israel: the disorders. The expositors observe the Dan sought an inheritance: to dwell in. The expositors observe the not yet possessed: their lot unwon. The expositors observe the disorder without rule: where there is no vision, the people perish (Prov. 29:18). The believer is taught to mark the no king in Israel, the Dan sought an inheritance, the not yet possessed, the disorder without rule, and to seek counsel of the LORD and not of idols. The verse thus records that there was no king in Israel and Dan sought an inheritance not yet possessed — the want of godly rule breeding disorder, the failure to possess God's gift breeding wandering, where there is no vision the people perish (Prov. 29:18).
2:
"And the children of Dan sent of their family five men... to spy out the land, and to search it; and they came to mount Ephraim, to the house of Micah, and lodged there." The witnesses note Dan sending five men to spy the land, who lodged at Micah's house. The witnesses note that Gill notes they sent five men, as Moses sent spies to search the land. The witnesses note that they came to mount Ephraim, to the house of Micah, and lodged there. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the five men — to spy the land; the mount Ephraim — their way; the the house of Micah — the idol's shrine; the the danger of the snare — a companion of fools is destroyed (Prov. 13:20).

The witnesses agree the Danites sent spies to seek a new home — "five men... to spy out the land... and lodged there" at Micah's house: bent on finding an easier inheritance, they searched the land and lodged where they would find the idolatrous shrine of Micah; the providence that brought them to that house would entangle them in its idolatry; it teaches that men's ways, even when seeking their own ends, are overruled to bring them where God will work out his purpose, yet that those who lodge near idolatry are in danger of its snare, "he that walketh with wise men shall be wise: but a companion of fools shall be destroyed" (Prov. 13:20), the spies lodging at the house of Micah.

So the witnesses make the verse lodged at Micah's: The witnesses note the five men: to spy the land. The witnesses note the mount Ephraim: their way. The witnesses note the the house of Micah: the idol's shrine. The witnesses note the the danger of the snare: a companion of fools is destroyed (Prov. 13:20). The expositors observe the five men: to spy the land. The expositors observe the mount Ephraim: their way. The expositors observe the the house of Micah: the idol's shrine. The expositors observe the the danger of the snare: a companion of fools is destroyed (Prov. 13:20). The believer is taught to mark the five men, the mount Ephraim, the the house of Micah, the the danger of the snare, and to seek counsel of the LORD and not of idols. The verse thus records that Dan sent five spies who lodged at Micah's house — men's ways overruled to God's purpose, yet those who lodge near idolatry in danger of its snare, a companion of fools destroyed (Prov. 13:20).
3:
"When they were by the house of Micah, they knew the voice of the young man the Levite: and they turned in thither, and said unto him, Who brought thee hither? and what makest thou in this place? and what hast thou here?" The witnesses note the spies recognising the young Levite and questioning his presence there. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were at their inn next to Micah's house, or passing by it. The witnesses note that they knew the voice of the young Levite and questioned him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the knew the Levite's voice — they recognised him; the who brought thee hither — the inquiry; the what makest thou here — the searching question; the the shame of his case — how is the faithful become an harlot (Isa. 1:21).

The witnesses agree the spies recognised the Levite and pressed him with searching questions — "Who brought thee hither? and what makest thou in this place? and what hast thou here?": their threefold inquiry probed how a Levite, set apart for the LORD's service, came to serve in a private idolatrous shrine; the questions, whether of curiosity or reproach, lay bare the strangeness of his case; it teaches that the servants of God are rightly questioned when found in unworthy employ, and that the Levite's wandering into hireling idolatry was a shame to his calling, "how is the faithful city become an harlot!" (Isa. 1:21) — the Levite questioned at Micah's house.

So the witnesses make the verse what makest thou here: The witnesses note the knew the Levite's voice: they recognised him. The witnesses note the who brought thee hither: the inquiry. The witnesses note the what makest thou here: the searching question. The witnesses note the the shame of his case: how is the faithful become an harlot (Isa. 1:21). The expositors observe the knew the Levite's voice: they recognised him. The expositors observe the who brought thee hither: the inquiry. The expositors observe the what makest thou here: the searching question. The expositors observe the the shame of his case: how is the faithful become an harlot (Isa. 1:21). The believer is taught to mark the knew the Levite's voice, the who brought thee hither, the what makest thou here, the the shame of his case, and to seek counsel of the LORD and not of idols. The verse thus records that the spies questioned the young Levite's presence at the shrine — the servant of God rightly questioned in unworthy employ, his wandering into hireling idolatry a shame to his calling, how is the faithful city become an harlot (Isa. 1:21).
4:
"And he said unto them, Thus and thus dealeth Micah with me, and hath hired me, and I am his priest." The witnesses note the Levite owning that Micah had hired him to be his priest. The witnesses note that Gill notes he told them the whole story, how Micah dealt with him. The witnesses note that he said, he hath hired me, and I am his priest. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thus dealeth Micah — the Levite's tale; the hath hired me — for wages; the I am his priest — the hireling; the merchandise of holy things — the hireling careth not for the sheep (John 10:13).

The witnesses agree the Levite confessed without shame that he was a hired priest — "Micah... hath hired me, and I am his priest": he had sold his sacred calling for wages, serving a private man's idols for hire; the unblushing avowal of a hireling ministry shows how low the priesthood had fallen in those lawless days; it teaches the shame of those who make merchandise of holy things and serve for gain rather than for God, the hireling who "careth not for the sheep" (John 10:13) — the Levite owning he was Micah's hired priest.

So the witnesses make the verse I am his priest: The witnesses note the thus dealeth Micah: the Levite's tale. The witnesses note the hath hired me: for wages. The witnesses note the I am his priest: the hireling. The witnesses note the merchandise of holy things: the hireling careth not for the sheep (John 10:13). The expositors observe the thus dealeth Micah: the Levite's tale. The expositors observe the hath hired me: for wages. The expositors observe the I am his priest: the hireling. The expositors observe the merchandise of holy things: the hireling careth not for the sheep (John 10:13). The believer is taught to mark the thus dealeth Micah, the hath hired me, the I am his priest, the merchandise of holy things, and to seek counsel of the LORD and not of idols. The verse thus records that the Levite owned Micah had hired him as his priest — the shame of a hireling ministry, those who make merchandise of holy things serving for gain not God, the hireling who careth not for the sheep (John 10:13).
5:
"And they said unto him, Ask counsel, we pray thee, of God, that we may know whether our way which we go shall be prosperous." The witnesses note the spies asking the idolatrous priest to inquire of God for them. The witnesses note that Gill notes they did not reprove him for assuming the priestly office, such was the corruption of those times. The witnesses note that they asked whether their way would be prosperous. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ask counsel of God — through the priest; the whether our way — shall prosper; the no reproof of the idol — the corruption of the times; the seek God aright — should not a people seek unto their God? (Isa. 8:19).

The witnesses agree the spies sought a word about their venture, but from a corrupt and idolatrous source — "Ask counsel... of God, that we may know whether our way... shall be prosperous": Gill notes the depravity of the times, that they neither reproved the false priest nor sought the LORD aright, but inquired by an idol; the seeking of guidance through idolatry shows how far religion had fallen; it teaches that men may have a form of seeking God while using means he abhors, and that the people of God must inquire of him in his appointed way, not through idols or false prophets, "should not a people seek unto their God?" (Isa. 8:19), the spies asking counsel through Micah's priest.

So the witnesses make the verse ask counsel of God: The witnesses note the ask counsel of God: through the priest. The witnesses note the whether our way: shall prosper. The witnesses note the no reproof of the idol: the corruption of the times. The witnesses note the seek God aright: should not a people seek unto their God? (Isa. 8:19). The expositors observe the ask counsel of God: through the priest. The expositors observe the whether our way: shall prosper. The expositors observe the no reproof of the idol: the corruption of the times. The expositors observe the seek God aright: should not a people seek unto their God? (Isa. 8:19). The believer is taught to mark the ask counsel of God, the whether our way, the no reproof of the idol, the seek God aright, and to seek counsel of the LORD and not of idols. The verse thus records that the spies asked the idolatrous priest to inquire of God — a form of seeking God while using means he abhors, the people of God to inquire in his appointed way, not through idols, should not a people seek unto their God? (Isa. 8:19).
6:
"And the priest said unto them, Go in peace: before the LORD is your way wherein ye go." The witnesses note the priest giving a presumptuous word of blessing on their way. The witnesses note that Gill notes he consulted the ephod and teraphim, or spoke of his own head, or by a voice Satan might be permitted to deliver. The witnesses note that he said, before the LORD is your way. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the go in peace — the smooth word; the before the LORD is your way — the oracle; the from the teraphim — a deceiving voice; the false prophets speak peace — they prophesy a false vision (Jer. 14:14).

The witnesses agree the false priest gave a smooth and presumptuous answer — "Go in peace: before the LORD is your way": Gill notes it may have come from his own head, or from the teraphim, or even by a deceiving voice Satan was permitted to give; the easy oracle, flattering their enterprise, shows the peril of false prophets who speak peace where God has not spoken; it teaches that men love a smooth word that approves their own designs, and that the lying oracle of idolatry cannot be trusted, "they prophesy unto you a false vision... and the deceit of their heart" (Jer. 14:14), the priest saying their way was before the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse go in peace: The witnesses note the go in peace: the smooth word. The witnesses note the before the LORD is your way: the oracle. The witnesses note the from the teraphim: a deceiving voice. The witnesses note the false prophets speak peace: they prophesy a false vision (Jer. 14:14). The expositors observe the go in peace: the smooth word. The expositors observe the before the LORD is your way: the oracle. The expositors observe the from the teraphim: a deceiving voice. The expositors observe the false prophets speak peace: they prophesy a false vision (Jer. 14:14). The believer is taught to mark the go in peace, the before the LORD is your way, the from the teraphim, the false prophets speak peace, and to seek counsel of the LORD and not of idols. The verse thus records that the priest gave a presumptuous word that their way was before the LORD — the peril of false prophets who speak peace where God has not spoken, the lying oracle of idolatry not to be trusted, prophesying a false vision and the deceit of their heart (Jer. 14:14).
7:
"Then the five men departed, and came to Laish, and saw the people that were therein... dwelt careless... quiet and secure... and there was no magistrate in the land..." The witnesses note the spies finding Laish a careless, quiet, and unguarded people. The witnesses note that Gill notes they came to Laish after viewing it well. The witnesses note that Henry notes the observation the spies made on the city and the posture of its inhabitants. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the came to Laish — the survey; the dwelt careless — quiet and secure; the no magistrate — none to defend; the exposed to ruin — sudden destruction in their peace (1 Thess. 5:3).

The witnesses agree the spies found Laish ripe for the taking, its people heedless and secure — "dwelt careless... quiet and secure... no magistrate in the land": far from help and without rule, the people lived at ease, unsuspecting of danger; the false security of Laish, with none to defend or warn, shows how the careless are exposed to sudden ruin; it teaches that ease and security without watchfulness are a snare, and that the people who dwell carelessly are overtaken unawares, "when they shall say, Peace and safety; then sudden destruction cometh upon them" (1 Thess. 5:3), Laish dwelling careless and secure.

So the witnesses make the verse dwelt careless: The witnesses note the came to Laish: the survey. The witnesses note the dwelt careless: quiet and secure. The witnesses note the no magistrate: none to defend. The witnesses note the exposed to ruin: sudden destruction in their peace (1 Thess. 5:3). The expositors observe the came to Laish: the survey. The expositors observe the dwelt careless: quiet and secure. The expositors observe the no magistrate: none to defend. The expositors observe the exposed to ruin: sudden destruction in their peace (1 Thess. 5:3). The believer is taught to mark the came to Laish, the dwelt careless, the no magistrate, the exposed to ruin, and to seek counsel of the LORD and not of idols. The verse thus records that the spies found Laish careless, quiet, and unguarded — the false security of the heedless exposing them to sudden ruin, ease without watchfulness a snare, sudden destruction coming on their peace (1 Thess. 5:3).
8:
"And they came unto their brethren to Zorah and Eshtaol: and their brethren said unto them, What say ye?" The witnesses note the spies returning to their brethren with their report. The witnesses note that Gill notes they returned after viewing the city and its circumstances. The witnesses note that their brethren said, What say ye? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the came to their brethren — at Zorah; the the report — to render; the what say ye — the eager question; the the foundation tainted — the sacrifice of the wicked is abomination (Prov. 21:27).

The witnesses agree the spies came back to render their report — "they came unto their brethren... What say ye?": the waiting tribe eagerly asked the outcome of the search; the bringing back of the report shows the orderly way of a venture taken upon inquiry; yet the whole enterprise, founded on a false oracle and bound up with idolatry, was tainted at the root; it teaches that even a well-ordered undertaking is corrupt if its foundation is in disobedience and idolatry, the fairest report not sanctifying an evil cause, "the sacrifice of the wicked is abomination" (Prov. 21:27), the spies returning to their brethren.

So the witnesses make the verse what say ye: The witnesses note the came to their brethren: at Zorah. The witnesses note the the report: to render. The witnesses note the what say ye: the eager question. The witnesses note the the foundation tainted: the sacrifice of the wicked is abomination (Prov. 21:27). The expositors observe the came to their brethren: at Zorah. The expositors observe the the report: to render. The expositors observe the what say ye: the eager question. The expositors observe the the foundation tainted: the sacrifice of the wicked is abomination (Prov. 21:27). The believer is taught to mark the came to their brethren, the the report, the what say ye, the the foundation tainted, and to seek counsel of the LORD and not of idols. The verse thus records that the spies returned to their brethren with their report — the orderly venture yet tainted at the root by a false oracle and idolatry, the fairest report not sanctifying an evil cause, the sacrifice of the wicked an abomination (Prov. 21:27).
9:
"And they said, Arise, that we may go up against them: for we have seen the land, and, behold, it is very good: and are ye still? be not slothful to go..." The witnesses note the spies urging their brethren to go up at once against the good land. The witnesses note that Gill notes they urged them to prepare for war and go up against the present possessors. The witnesses note that they said, be not slothful to go. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the arise, go up — against them; the it is very good — the rich land; the are ye still — their urging; the be not slothful — followers who inherit the promises (Heb. 6:12).

The witnesses agree the spies pressed their brethren to act without delay — "Arise... it is very good... be not slothful to go": they urged a swift seizing of the rich and undefended land; their zeal and energy in this earthly enterprise, set against the slackness of God's people in spiritual things, is a rebuke; it teaches that the children of this world are often more diligent in their own affairs than the children of light in the things of God, and that the energy spent on earthly gain ought rather to be given to possessing the heavenly inheritance, "be not slothful, but followers of them who through faith... inherit the promises" (Heb. 6:12), the spies urging Dan to go up.

So the witnesses make the verse be not slothful: The witnesses note the arise, go up: against them. The witnesses note the it is very good: the rich land. The witnesses note the are ye still: their urging. The witnesses note the be not slothful: followers who inherit the promises (Heb. 6:12). The expositors observe the arise, go up: against them. The expositors observe the it is very good: the rich land. The expositors observe the are ye still: their urging. The expositors observe the be not slothful: followers who inherit the promises (Heb. 6:12). The believer is taught to mark the arise, go up, the it is very good, the are ye still, the be not slothful, and to seek counsel of the LORD and not of idols. The verse thus records that the spies urged their brethren to seize the good land without delay — the diligence of the world in earthly gain a rebuke to the slackness of God's people, be not slothful but followers of them who inherit the promises (Heb. 6:12).
10:
"When ye go, ye shall come unto a people secure, and to a large land: for God hath given it into your hands; a place where there is no want of any thing that is in the earth." The witnesses note the spies claiming God had given the land into their hands. The witnesses note that Gill notes the territories were large and the people secure, easily mastered. The witnesses note that they said, a place where there is no want of any thing. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a people secure — a large land; the God hath given it — their presumption; the no want of any thing — the abundance; the presuming on his name — they have not asked at my mouth (Isa. 30:2).

The witnesses agree the spies clothed their covetous enterprise in the language of God's blessing — "God hath given it into your hands; a place where there is no want": they presumed upon a word the LORD had not truly given, founding their confidence on a false oracle; the using of God's name to sanction a venture born of idolatry shows how men presume on his blessing for their own designs; it teaches that men must not take the LORD's name to bless what he has not appointed, and that the abundance promised by covetousness is a snare, "they... have not asked at my mouth" (Isa. 30:2), the spies claiming God had given them the land.

So the witnesses make the verse God hath given it: The witnesses note the a people secure: a large land. The witnesses note the God hath given it: their presumption. The witnesses note the no want of any thing: the abundance. The witnesses note the presuming on his name: they have not asked at my mouth (Isa. 30:2). The expositors observe the a people secure: a large land. The expositors observe the God hath given it: their presumption. The expositors observe the no want of any thing: the abundance. The expositors observe the presuming on his name: they have not asked at my mouth (Isa. 30:2). The believer is taught to mark the a people secure, the God hath given it, the no want of any thing, the presuming on his name, and to seek counsel of the LORD and not of idols. The verse thus records that the spies claimed God had given the land into their hands — presuming upon a word he had not given, founding their confidence on a false oracle, men not to take his name to bless what he has not appointed, having not asked at his mouth (Isa. 30:2).
11:
"And there went from thence of the family of the Danites, out of Zorah and out of Eshtaol, six hundred men appointed with weapons of war." The witnesses note six hundred armed Danites setting out for the conquest. The witnesses note that Gill notes the singular is put for the plural, for such a number could hardly come from one family. The witnesses note that they were six hundred men appointed with weapons of war. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the of the family of Dan — out of Zorah; the six hundred men — a strong band; the weapons of war — in force; the might not right — imputing his power to his god (Hab. 1:11).

The witnesses agree the tribe sent a strong armed band — "six hundred men appointed with weapons of war": they went forth in force to seize the land and, as appears, the idols of Micah by the way; the marching of an armed troop to take both land and gods by might shows the lawless violence of the age, when there was no king and might made right; it teaches that where the fear of God and lawful rule are cast off, men take what they will by force, and that the strength of numbers is no warrant for an unjust cause, "their might is not right" (cf. Hab. 1:11, imputing this his power unto his god), the six hundred armed Danites setting out.

So the witnesses make the verse six hundred armed: The witnesses note the of the family of Dan: out of Zorah. The witnesses note the six hundred men: a strong band. The witnesses note the weapons of war: in force. The witnesses note the might not right: imputing his power to his god (Hab. 1:11). The expositors observe the of the family of Dan: out of Zorah. The expositors observe the six hundred men: a strong band. The expositors observe the weapons of war: in force. The expositors observe the might not right: imputing his power to his god (Hab. 1:11). The believer is taught to mark the of the family of Dan, the six hundred men, the weapons of war, the might not right, and to flee the love of gain and the worship of idols. The verse thus records that six hundred armed Danites set out for the conquest — the lawless violence of an age without a king, men taking what they will by force, might no warrant for an unjust cause (Hab. 1:11).
12:
"And they went up, and pitched in Kirjathjearim, in Judah: wherefore they called that place Mahanehdan unto this day..." The witnesses note the Danites pitching at Kirjath-jearim, which they named Mahaneh-dan, the camp of Dan. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was sixteen miles from Zorah, their first day's march. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them naming it Mahaneh-dan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the pitched in Kirjath-jearim — in Judah; the called it Mahaneh-dan — the camp of Dan; the the march recorded — their passing; the self-willed exploits — they called their lands after their names (Ps. 49:11).

The witnesses agree the march left its mark in the name of the place — "they... pitched in Kirjathjearim... they called that place Mahanehdan, the camp of Dan": the naming of the spot recorded their passing; yet the whole expedition, bent on seizing land and idols, was no honourable march but a venture of covetousness; the memorial of so doubtful an enterprise teaches that not every venture men commemorate is worthy, and that the people of God should mark the scenes of his mercies rather than their own self-willed exploits, "they called their lands after their own names" (Ps. 49:11), the Danites naming Mahaneh-dan.

So the witnesses make the verse Mahaneh-dan: The witnesses note the pitched in Kirjath-jearim: in Judah. The witnesses note the called it Mahaneh-dan: the camp of Dan. The witnesses note the the march recorded: their passing. The witnesses note the self-willed exploits: they called their lands after their names (Ps. 49:11). The expositors observe the pitched in Kirjath-jearim: in Judah. The expositors observe the called it Mahaneh-dan: the camp of Dan. The expositors observe the the march recorded: their passing. The expositors observe the self-willed exploits: they called their lands after their names (Ps. 49:11). The believer is taught to mark the pitched in Kirjath-jearim, the called it Mahaneh-dan, the the march recorded, the self-willed exploits, and to flee the love of gain and the worship of idols. The verse thus records that the Danites named their camp Mahaneh-dan — the memorial of a doubtful, covetous enterprise, not every venture men commemorate worthy, men calling their lands after their own names (Ps. 49:11).
13:
"And they passed thence unto mount Ephraim, and came unto the house of Micah." The witnesses note the Danites coming to the house of Micah on their northward march. The witnesses note that Gill notes they steered their course northward, eight miles from Kirjath-jearim. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them coming to the house of Micah. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the passed to mount Ephraim — northward; the came unto the house of Micah — the shrine; the the place of idolatry — for the theft; the one sin draws another — evil pursueth sinners (Prov. 13:21).

The witnesses agree the march brought the band to the very house of idolatry — "they... came unto the house of Micah": the providence that led them by the shrine would draw them into the theft of its idols; the coming of the armed troop to the place of false worship set the scene for the robbery to follow; it teaches that the paths of the covetous lead them to the occasions of further sin, one evil drawing on another, and that those who set out on a self-willed course are carried from one transgression to the next, "evil pursueth sinners" (Prov. 13:21), the Danites coming to the house of Micah.

So the witnesses make the verse the house of Micah: The witnesses note the passed to mount Ephraim: northward. The witnesses note the came unto the house of Micah: the shrine. The witnesses note the the place of idolatry: for the theft. The witnesses note the one sin draws another: evil pursueth sinners (Prov. 13:21). The expositors observe the passed to mount Ephraim: northward. The expositors observe the came unto the house of Micah: the shrine. The expositors observe the the place of idolatry: for the theft. The expositors observe the one sin draws another: evil pursueth sinners (Prov. 13:21). The believer is taught to mark the passed to mount Ephraim, the came unto the house of Micah, the the place of idolatry, the one sin draws another, and to flee the love of gain and the worship of idols. The verse thus records that the Danites came to the house of Micah — the path of the covetous leading to the occasions of further sin, one evil drawing on another, evil pursuing sinners (Prov. 13:21).
14:
"Then answered the five men... and said unto their brethren, Do ye know that there is in these houses an ephod, and teraphim, and a graven image, and a molten image? now therefore consider what ye have to do." The witnesses note the spies telling of the idols in Micah's house and prompting the theft. The witnesses note that Gill notes the five men had some notion of Laish as proper for them. The witnesses note that they said, consider what ye have to do. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the five men answered — their brethren; the an ephod and teraphim — the idols; the a graven and molten image — to seize; the gain mistaken for godliness — supposing gain is godliness (1 Tim. 6:5).

The witnesses agree the spies, who had seen the idols, now stirred their brethren to seize them — "there is in these houses an ephod, and teraphim... now... consider what ye have to do": they would carry off the false gods to set up their own worship in the new land; the deliberate planning of the theft of idols for their use shows how the covetous heart adds sacrilege to robbery; it teaches that men sunk in idolatry will steal and plunder for their false worship, mistaking gain for godliness, "supposing that gain is godliness: from such withdraw thyself" (1 Tim. 6:5), the spies prompting the seizing of Micah's idols.

So the witnesses make the verse consider what to do: The witnesses note the the five men answered: their brethren. The witnesses note the an ephod and teraphim: the idols. The witnesses note the a graven and molten image: to seize. The witnesses note the gain mistaken for godliness: supposing gain is godliness (1 Tim. 6:5). The expositors observe the the five men answered: their brethren. The expositors observe the an ephod and teraphim: the idols. The expositors observe the a graven and molten image: to seize. The expositors observe the gain mistaken for godliness: supposing gain is godliness (1 Tim. 6:5). The believer is taught to mark the the five men answered, the an ephod and teraphim, the a graven and molten image, the gain mistaken for godliness, and to flee the love of gain and the worship of idols. The verse thus records that the spies prompted the seizing of Micah's idols — the covetous heart adding sacrilege to robbery, men sunk in idolatry plundering for their false worship, supposing gain is godliness (1 Tim. 6:5).
15:
"And they turned thitherward, and came to the house of the young man the Levite, even unto the house of Micah, and saluted him." The witnesses note the spies turning aside to the Levite's lodging and greeting him. The witnesses note that Gill notes the house lay a little out of their way, so they turned to it. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them coming to the house and saluting him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the turned thitherward — to the house; the the young Levite — his lodging; the saluted him — the friendly mask; the guile cloaked in words — his words softer than oil, yet drawn swords (Ps. 55:21).

The witnesses agree the spies came with a friendly salute to disarm the Levite — "they turned thitherward... and saluted him": the courteous greeting masked their purpose to rob the house of its idols and draw away its priest; the friendly approach that cloaked an evil design shows the guile with which the wicked compass their ends; it teaches that fair words may hide a foul intent, and that the people of God must beware the flattering approach that conceals a snare, "his words were softer than oil, yet were they drawn swords" (Ps. 55:21), the spies saluting the Levite.

So the witnesses make the verse saluted him: The witnesses note the turned thitherward: to the house. The witnesses note the the young Levite: his lodging. The witnesses note the saluted him: the friendly mask. The witnesses note the guile cloaked in words: his words softer than oil, yet drawn swords (Ps. 55:21). The expositors observe the turned thitherward: to the house. The expositors observe the the young Levite: his lodging. The expositors observe the saluted him: the friendly mask. The expositors observe the guile cloaked in words: his words softer than oil, yet drawn swords (Ps. 55:21). The believer is taught to mark the turned thitherward, the the young Levite, the saluted him, the guile cloaked in words, and to flee the love of gain and the worship of idols. The verse thus records that the spies turned aside and saluted the Levite — the friendly greeting masking their design to rob and draw him away, fair words hiding a foul intent, words softer than oil yet drawn swords (Ps. 55:21).
16:
"And the six hundred men appointed with their weapons of war, which were of the children of Dan, stood by the entering of the gate." The witnesses note the six hundred armed men standing at the gate while the idols were taken. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were armed men, marching with their armour about them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them standing by the entering of the gate. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the six hundred — armed men; the weapons of war — to overawe; the stood at the gate — the menace; the the strong against the weak — the violent purposed to overthrow my goings (Ps. 140:4).

The witnesses agree the armed band stood ready to back the theft by force — "the six hundred men... stood by the entering of the gate": their menacing presence at the gate ensured that none could resist the plundering of Micah's house; the using of armed might to overawe a single household shows the oppression of the strong against the weak in lawless times; it teaches that the wicked use their power to crush resistance and take what is not theirs, and that the people of God must trust the LORD against the violence of the mighty, "the violent man... they have purposed to overthrow my goings" (Ps. 140:4), the six hundred standing at the gate.

So the witnesses make the verse stood at the gate: The witnesses note the the six hundred: armed men. The witnesses note the weapons of war: to overawe. The witnesses note the stood at the gate: the menace. The witnesses note the the strong against the weak: the violent purposed to overthrow my goings (Ps. 140:4). The expositors observe the the six hundred: armed men. The expositors observe the weapons of war: to overawe. The expositors observe the stood at the gate: the menace. The expositors observe the the strong against the weak: the violent purposed to overthrow my goings (Ps. 140:4). The believer is taught to mark the the six hundred, the weapons of war, the stood at the gate, the the strong against the weak, and to flee the love of gain and the worship of idols. The verse thus records that the six hundred armed men stood at the gate to back the theft — the oppression of the strong against the weak in lawless times, the wicked using power to take what is not theirs, the violent purposing to overthrow (Ps. 140:4).
17:
"And the five men that went to spy out the land went up, and came in thither, and took the graven image, and the ephod, and the teraphim, and the molten image..." The witnesses note the five spies taking the graven image, ephod, teraphim, and molten image. The witnesses note that Gill notes they first came to Micah's house and saluted the young man. The witnesses note that they took the graven image, ephod, teraphim, and molten image. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the five men went up — into the house; the the graven image — and ephod; the the teraphim — and molten image; the idols cannot save — they must needs be borne, they cannot deliver (Jer. 10:5).

The witnesses agree the theft was carried out while the priest was kept at the gate — "the five men... took the graven image, and the ephod, and the teraphim, and the molten image": they plundered the whole apparatus of Micah's false worship; the carrying-off of gods that could not defend themselves or their own house exposes the impotence of idols; it teaches the vanity of false gods, which must be carried, guarded, and yet are stolen, unable to save their worshippers or themselves, "they must needs be borne, because they cannot go... they cannot deliver" (Jer. 10:5), the spies taking Micah's idols.

So the witnesses make the verse took the images: The witnesses note the the five men went up: into the house. The witnesses note the the graven image: and ephod. The witnesses note the the teraphim: and molten image. The witnesses note the idols cannot save: they must needs be borne, they cannot deliver (Jer. 10:5). The expositors observe the the five men went up: into the house. The expositors observe the the graven image: and ephod. The expositors observe the the teraphim: and molten image. The verse thus records that the five spies took Micah's graven image, ephod, teraphim, and molten image — the impotence of idols that cannot defend themselves, the vanity of false gods that must be carried and cannot deliver (Jer. 10:5).
18:
"And these went into Micah's house, and fetched the carved image, the ephod, and the teraphim, and the molten image. Then said the priest unto them, What do ye?" The witnesses note the priest seeing the idols taken and asking what they did. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was the five men who knew the house and the chapel where the things were. The witnesses note that the priest said, What do ye? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the went into the house — for the idols; the fetched the images — the plunder; the the priest said — what do ye?; the self-interest, not truth — every one for his gain (Isa. 56:11).

The witnesses agree the priest at last protested the plunder — "Then said the priest... What do ye?": seeing the gods carried off, he objected, though feebly; his question shows a conscience stirred too late, and only for the loss of his place and gain, not for the sin of idolatry; it teaches that the hireling cares for his wages more than for the truth, and that a conscience awakened only by self-interest is no true religion, "they all look to their own way, every one for his gain" (Isa. 56:11), the priest asking what they did.

So the witnesses make the verse what do ye: The witnesses note the went into the house: for the idols. The witnesses note the fetched the images: the plunder. The witnesses note the the priest said: What do ye? The witnesses note the self-interest, not truth: every one for his gain (Isa. 56:11). The expositors observe the went into the house: for the idols. The expositors observe the fetched the images: the plunder. The expositors observe the the priest said: What do ye? The verse thus records that the priest protested the plunder, asking what they did — a conscience stirred too late and only for his own loss, the hireling caring for his wages more than the truth, every one for his gain (Isa. 56:11).
19:
"And they said unto him, Hold thy peace, lay thine hand upon thy mouth, and go with us, and be to us a father and a priest: is it better... to be a priest unto the house of one man, or... unto a tribe and a family in Israel?" The witnesses note the Danites silencing the priest and enticing him with greater preferment. The witnesses note that Gill notes they bid him be silent and quiet. The witnesses note that Clarke notes laying the hand on the mouth was the token of silence. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the hold thy peace — silenced; the go with us — the enticement; the a father and a priest — greater office; the the lure of preferment — they that will be rich fall into a snare (1 Tim. 6:9).

The witnesses agree the Danites silenced the priest and bought him with ambition — "Hold thy peace... go with us... is it better to be a priest unto one man, or unto a tribe?": they appealed to his love of advancement, offering a wider sphere and greater gain; the enticing of the Levite by the lure of preferment shows how ambition and covetousness draw men from one false service to a larger one; it teaches that the love of position and gain corrupts the ministry, and that he who serves for advancement will follow it wherever it leads, "they that will be rich fall into... a snare" (1 Tim. 6:9), the Danites enticing the priest to go with them.

So the witnesses make the verse a father and a priest: The witnesses note the hold thy peace: silenced. The witnesses note the go with us: the enticement. The witnesses note the a father and a priest: greater office. The witnesses note the the lure of preferment: they that will be rich fall into a snare (1 Tim. 6:9). The expositors observe the hold thy peace: silenced. The expositors observe the go with us: the enticement. The expositors observe the a father and a priest: greater office. The expositors observe the the lure of preferment: they that will be rich fall into a snare (1 Tim. 6:9). The believer is taught to mark the hold thy peace, the go with us, the a father and a priest, the the lure of preferment, and to flee the love of gain and the worship of idols. The verse thus records that the Danites silenced the priest and enticed him with greater preferment — ambition and covetousness drawing men from one false service to a larger, the love of gain corrupting the ministry, they that will be rich falling into a snare (1 Tim. 6:9).
20:
"And the priest's heart was glad, and he took the ephod, and the teraphim, and the graven image, and went in the midst of the people." The witnesses note the priest gladly taking the idols and joining the Danites. The witnesses note that Gill notes he rejoiced at the opportunity, suiting his covetous, ambitious, unsettled disposition. The witnesses note that he went in the midst of the people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the his heart was glad — at the offer; the took the idols — he had served; the went in the midst — with the thieves; the ruled by gain — the love of money the root of all evil (1 Tim. 6:10).

The witnesses agree the priest gladly betrayed his master for a better place — "the priest's heart was glad... and went in the midst of the people": Gill notes the offer "suited his covetous, ambitious... unsettled disposition"; forsaking Micah, he took the very idols he had served and went off with the thieves; the gladness of the hireling at his own advancement, careless of faith or gratitude, shows the baseness of a heart ruled by gain; it teaches that the love of money makes men faithless and shameless, ready to forsake all for advantage, "the love of money is the root of all evil" (1 Tim. 6:10), the priest glad and going with the Danites.

So the witnesses make the verse the priest's heart was glad: The witnesses note the his heart was glad: at the offer. The witnesses note the took the idols: he had served. The witnesses note the went in the midst: with the thieves. The witnesses note the ruled by gain: the love of money the root of all evil (1 Tim. 6:10). 21:
"So they turned and departed, and put the little ones and the cattle and the carriage before them." The witnesses note the Danites departing with their little ones, cattle, and goods before them. The witnesses note that Gill notes they turned from the gate and marched forward to Laish. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them putting the little ones and cattle before them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the turned and departed — toward Laish; the the little ones — before them; the the cattle and carriage — guarded; the the guilty fear pursuit — the wicked flee when none pursueth (Prov. 28:1).

The witnesses agree the Danites marched on with the stolen idols and their families — "they... put the little ones and the cattle and the carriage before them": they set their weakest in front, the armed men behind, to guard against pursuit; the careful ordering of their march shows their guilty awareness that they might be followed; it teaches that those who do wrong go in fear of the consequence, looking over their shoulder for the avenger, "the wicked flee when no man pursueth" (Prov. 28:1), the Danites departing with their goods before them.

So the witnesses make the verse departed with their goods: The witnesses note the turned and departed: toward Laish. The witnesses note the the little ones: before them. The witnesses note the the cattle and carriage: guarded. The witnesses note the the guilty fear pursuit: the wicked flee when none pursueth (Prov. 28:1). 22:
"And when they were a good way from the house of Micah, the men that were in the houses near to Micah's house were gathered together, and overtook the children of Dan." The witnesses note Micah gathering his neighbours to pursue the Danites who had robbed him. The witnesses note that Gill notes it took time before Micah knew his gods were stolen and his priest decoyed away. The witnesses note that the men near Micah's house overtook the Danites. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a good way off — they pursued; the the men near Micah — gathered; the overtook the Danites — to recover the idols; the the idolater's vain trust — they that make a graven image are vanity (Isa. 44:9).

The witnesses agree Micah, robbed of his gods, raised his neighbours to pursue — "the men... near to Micah's house were gathered together, and overtook the children of Dan": he could not bear the loss of the idols he trusted, and rallied help to recover them; the eagerness to recover his false gods shows how the idolater's heart is set on his vain trust; it teaches the folly of the man who pursues after idols as his treasure, his heart bound up in what cannot help him, "they that make a graven image are all of them vanity" (Isa. 44:9), Micah overtaking the Danites to recover his gods.

So the witnesses make the verse overtook the Danites: The witnesses note the a good way off: they pursued. The witnesses note the the men near Micah: gathered. The witnesses note the overtook the Danites: to recover the idols. The witnesses note the the idolater's vain trust: they that make a graven image are vanity (Isa. 44:9). 23:
"And they cried unto the children of Dan. And they turned their faces, and said unto Micah, What aileth thee, that thou comest with such a company?" The witnesses note the Danites coolly asking Micah what ailed him to pursue them. The witnesses note that Gill notes they called aloud, desiring them to stop. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the Danites asking, What aileth thee? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the they cried unto Dan — to stop them; the they turned their faces — coolly; the what aileth thee — feigned surprise; the insolence in sin — she saith, I have done no wickedness (Prov. 30:20).

The witnesses agree the Danites met Micah's pursuit with cold effrontery — "What aileth thee, that thou comest with such a company?": as if they had done no wrong, they feigned surprise at his coming; the brazen question of the thieves, pretending innocence, shows the hardness of those who sin without shame; it teaches that the wicked add insolence to wrongdoing, denying or making light of the very evil they have done, "such is the way of an adulterous woman... she... saith, I have done no wickedness" (Prov. 30:20), the Danites asking Micah what ailed him.

So the witnesses make the verse what aileth thee: The witnesses note the they cried unto Dan: to stop them. The witnesses note the they turned their faces: coolly. The witnesses note the what aileth thee: feigned surprise. The witnesses note the insolence in sin: she saith, I have done no wickedness (Prov. 30:20). 24:
"And he said, Ye have taken away my gods which I made, and the priest, and ye are gone away: and what have I more? and what is this that ye say unto me, What aileth thee?" The witnesses note Micah's pitiful complaint that his gods and priest were taken, and he had nothing left. The witnesses note that Gill notes he meant his graven and molten images, which he had made or paid for. The witnesses note that he said, what have I more? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ye have taken my gods — which I made; the and the priest — carried off; the what have I more — his all gone; the the emptiness of idols — they are vanity... they shall perish (Jer. 10:15).

The witnesses agree Micah's lament lays bare the folly of idolatry — "Ye have taken away my gods which I made... and what have I more?": he mourned for gods of his own making, now carried off, as if his all were gone; the very words "my gods which I made" expose the absurdity of worshipping what one's own hands have fashioned; the cry "what have I more?" of a man whose treasure was an idol teaches that those who set their hearts on false gods are left desolate when these fail, having nothing in the true God; it teaches the emptiness of trusting in idols, "they are vanity, and the work of errors: in the time of their visitation they shall perish" (Jer. 10:15), Micah bereft of his gods.

So the witnesses make the verse my gods which I made: The witnesses note the ye have taken my gods: which I made. The witnesses note the and the priest: carried off. The witnesses note the what have I more: his all gone. The witnesses note the the emptiness of idols: they are vanity... they shall perish (Jer. 10:15). The expositors observe the ye have taken my gods: which I made. The expositors observe the and the priest: carried off. The expositors observe the what have I more: his all gone. The expositors observe the the emptiness of idols: they are vanity... they shall perish (Jer. 10:15). 25:
"And the children of Dan said unto him, Let not thy voice be heard among us, lest angry fellows run upon thee, and thou lose thy life, with the lives of thy household." The witnesses note the Danites threatening Micah's life if he would not be silent. The witnesses note that Gill notes they bid him not complain of them as having done an unjust thing. The witnesses note that they threatened his life and his household. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the let not thy voice be heard — the threat; the angry fellows — the menace; the thou lose thy life — and thy household; the violence after theft — wasting and destruction in their paths (Isa. 59:7).

The witnesses agree the Danites silenced Micah with a threat of death — "Let not thy voice be heard among us, lest... thou lose thy life, with the lives of thy household": having robbed him, they would now murder him if he protested; the adding of menace to theft shows the lawless violence of those who fear neither God nor man; it teaches that the wicked, having done wrong, will silence and crush any who resist, and that the people of God in evil times are at the mercy of the violent, "their feet run to evil... wasting and destruction are in their paths" (Isa. 59:7), the Danites threatening Micah's life.

So the witnesses make the verse lest thou lose thy life: The witnesses note the let not thy voice be heard: the threat. The witnesses note the angry fellows: the menace. The witnesses note the thou lose thy life: and thy household. The witnesses note the violence after theft: wasting and destruction in their paths (Isa. 59:7). The expositors observe the let not thy voice be heard: the threat. The expositors observe the angry fellows: the menace. The expositors observe the thou lose thy life: and thy household. The expositors observe the violence after theft: wasting and destruction in their paths (Isa. 59:7). 26:
"And the children of Dan went their way: and when Micah saw that they were too strong for him, he turned and went back unto his house." The witnesses note Micah, seeing the Danites too strong, turning back home empty. The witnesses note that Gill notes they bid him defiance, and Micah, being too weak, returned. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Micah turning back to his house. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Danites went their way — with the spoil; the too strong for him — outmatched; the turned back — empty and helpless; the the bankruptcy of idols — the idols he shall utterly abolish (Isa. 2:17-18).

The witnesses agree Micah, outmatched, gave up and went home robbed — "when Micah saw that they were too strong for him, he turned and went back unto his house": he could not recover his gods, and returned empty and helpless; the sorry end of the idolater, left with neither his idols nor any true God, shows the bankruptcy of false worship; it teaches that the man who builds his religion on idols is left with nothing in the day of loss, his vain confidence stripped away, "the LORD alone shall be exalted in that day. And the idols he shall utterly abolish" (Isa. 2:17-18), Micah turning back home robbed of his gods.

So the witnesses make the verse too strong for him: The witnesses note the the Danites went their way: with the spoil. The witnesses note the too strong for him: outmatched. The witnesses note the turned back: empty and helpless. The witnesses note the the bankruptcy of idols: the idols he shall utterly abolish (Isa. 2:17-18). 27:
"And they took the things which Micah had made, and the priest which he had, and came unto Laish, unto a people that were at quiet and secure: and they smote them with the edge of the sword, and burnt the city with fire." The witnesses note the Danites taking Laish, smiting the quiet people, and burning the city. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Danites kept the things Micah made and went on with them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them smiting Laish and burning the city. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took the things Micah made — and the priest; the a people at quiet — and secure; the smote them with the sword — and burnt the city; the security without God — folden as thorns, they shall be devoured (Nah. 1:10).

The witnesses agree the Danites fell upon the unsuspecting city — "came unto Laish, unto a people that were at quiet and secure: and they smote them... and burnt the city": the careless, defenceless people were cut down without warning; the sudden destruction of the secure shows how those who dwell at ease without God are overtaken in a moment; it teaches that earthly security without the LORD is no safety, and that the people who trust in their peace are swept away unawares, "while they be folden together as thorns... they shall be devoured" (Nah. 1:10), the Danites smiting and burning Laish.

The witnesses note the smote them with the sword: and burnt the city. The witnesses note the security without God: folden as thorns, they shall be devoured (Nah. 1:10). 28:
"And there was no deliverer, because it was far from Zidon, and they had no business with any man... And they built a city, and dwelt therein." The witnesses note Laish having no deliverer, being far from help, and the Danites building the city. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was far from Zidon, under whose protection they were, who could not help suddenly. The witnesses note that they built a city and dwelt therein. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no deliverer — none to save; the far from Zidon — cut off from help; the no business with any — isolated; the flee to the LORD — there is none to help (Ps. 22:11).

The witnesses agree the helplessness of Laish was complete — "there was no deliverer, because it was far from Zidon": cut off from any help, the people perished with none to save; the having "no deliverer" in the day of ruin is the saddest of states; it teaches the misery of those who, far from God, have no saviour in their extremity, and bids us flee to the LORD who is ever near to deliver, lest we be found with "no deliverer" like Laish, "there is none to help" (cf. Ps. 22:11), Laish with no deliverer and the Danites building there.

So the witnesses make the verse no deliverer: The witnesses note the no deliverer: none to save. The witnesses note the far from Zidon: cut off from help. The witnesses note the no business with any: isolated. The witnesses note the flee to the LORD: there is none to help (Ps. 22:11). The expositors observe the no deliverer: none to save. 29:
"And they called the name of the city Dan, after the name of Dan their father... howbeit the name of the city was Laish at the first." The witnesses note the Danites naming the city Dan, after their father, where Laish had been. The witnesses note that Gill notes they named it to show that, though at the furthest part of the land, they were of the tribe of Dan. The witnesses note that the name was Laish at the first. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the called the name Dan — after their father; the Laish at the first — renamed; the the northern bound — from Dan to Beersheba; the soon a seat of idolatry — they changed their glory into an ox (Ps. 106:20).

The witnesses agree the conquered city took the tribe's name — "they called the name of the city Dan... howbeit the name... was Laish at the first": the new name marked their possession, fixing the northern bound of Israel, so that "from Dan to Beersheba" became the span of the land; yet this Dan would become a byword for idolatry, where the graven image was set up and later Jeroboam's calf; the naming of a city soon notorious for false worship teaches that a place may bear an honoured name yet be defiled by sin, and warns that the form of belonging to God's people is no safeguard against apostasy, "they... changed their glory into the similitude of an ox" (Ps. 106:20), the city named Dan.

So the witnesses make the verse named Dan: The witnesses note the called the name Dan: after their father. The witnesses note the Laish at the first: renamed. The witnesses note the the northern bound: from Dan to Beersheba. The witnesses note the soon a seat of idolatry: they changed their glory into an ox (Ps. 106:20). The expositors observe the called the name Dan: after their father. The expositors observe the Laish at the first: renamed. The expositors observe the the northern bound: from Dan to Beersheba. The expositors observe the soon a seat of idolatry: they changed their glory into an ox (Ps. 106:20). The believer is taught to mark the called the name Dan, the Laish at the first, the the northern bound, the soon a seat of idolatry, and to forsake the idols that cannot save. The verse thus records that the Danites named the city Dan, where Laish had been — a place soon notorious for idolatry, a name honoured yet defiled by sin, the form of belonging no safeguard against apostasy, changing their glory into the similitude of an ox (Ps. 106:20).
30:
"And the children of Dan set up the graven image: and Jonathan, the son of Gershom, the son of Manasseh, he and his sons were priests to the tribe of Dan until the day of the captivity of the land." The witnesses note the Danites setting up the graven image, with a descendant of Moses as their idol-priest until the captivity. The witnesses note that Gill notes very probably a house was built for it, in the same place where Jeroboam later set up a golden calf. The witnesses note that Jonathan and his sons were priests to Dan until the captivity. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the set up the graven image — at Dan; the Jonathan, son of Gershom — grandson of Moses; the priests to Dan — until the captivity; the grace not inherited — the soul that sinneth shall die (Ezek. 18:20).

The witnesses agree the chapter reaches its darkest point in the setting-up of idolatry by the line of Moses — "the children of Dan set up the graven image: and Jonathan, the son of Gershom, the son of Manasseh (Moses)... and his sons were priests to the tribe of Dan until the captivity": a grandson of Moses himself became the priest of an idol, and his descendants after him, the corruption lasting "until the captivity"; the descent of even the holy line into idol-priesthood shows that no lineage secures against apostasy, and that this Dan became the very place of Jeroboam's calf; it teaches that grace is not inherited and that the children of the godly may fall to the deepest sin, "the soul that sinneth, it shall die... the son shall not bear the iniquity of the father" (Ezek. 18:20), the graven image set up at Dan.

So the witnesses make the verse set up the graven image: The witnesses note the set up the graven image: at Dan. The witnesses note the Jonathan, son of Gershom: grandson of Moses. The witnesses note the priests to Dan: until the captivity. The witnesses note the grace not inherited: the soul that sinneth shall die (Ezek. 18:20). The expositors observe the set up the graven image: at Dan. The expositors observe the Jonathan, son of Gershom: grandson of Moses. The expositors observe the priests to Dan: until the captivity. The expositors observe the grace not inherited: the soul that sinneth shall die (Ezek. 18:20). The believer is taught to mark the set up the graven image, the Jonathan, son of Gershom, the priests to Dan, the grace not inherited, and to forsake the idols that cannot save. The verse thus records that the Danites set up the graven image, with a descendant of Moses as their idol-priest until the captivity — no lineage securing against apostasy, the holy line fallen to the deepest sin, the soul that sinneth, it shall die (Ezek. 18:20).
31:
"And they set them up Micah's graven image, which he made, all the time that the house of God was in Shiloh." The witnesses note Micah's idol kept up at Dan all the while the house of God stood at Shiloh. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is repeated for the sake of the time of its continuance. The witnesses note that it stood all the time the house of God was in Shiloh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the set up Micah's image — at Dan; the which he made — the false worship; the all the time — it continued; the rival to the true — ye cannot drink the cup of the Lord and of devils (1 Cor. 10:21).

The witnesses agree the chapter closes on the sad coexistence of idolatry with the true worship — "they set them up Micah's graven image... all the time that the house of God was in Shiloh": while the true sanctuary of God stood at Shiloh, this rival idol-worship flourished at Dan; the standing of a false worship alongside the true shows the divided and corrupt state of religion in those days; it teaches that men may keep up an idol while the house of God is near at hand, preferring their own invention to his appointed worship, and warns against the rival altars of the heart, "ye cannot drink the cup of the Lord, and the cup of devils" (1 Cor. 10:21); thus the dark appendix closes, Micah's image set up while God's house was in Shiloh.

So the witnesses make the verse while the house of God was in Shiloh: The witnesses note the set up Micah's image: at Dan. The witnesses note the which he made: the false worship. The witnesses note the all the time: it continued. The witnesses note the rival to the true: ye cannot drink the cup of the Lord and of devils (1 Cor. 10:21). The expositors observe the set up Micah's image: at Dan. The expositors observe the which he made: the false worship. The expositors observe the all the time: it continued. The expositors observe the rival to the true: ye cannot drink the cup of the Lord and of devils (1 Cor. 10:21). The believer is taught to mark the set up Micah's image, the which he made, the all the time, the rival to the true, and to forsake the idols that cannot save. The verse thus records that Micah's idol stood at Dan all the while God's house was at Shiloh — the sad coexistence of idolatry with the true worship, men preferring their own invention to God's appointed way, who cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of devils (1 Cor. 10:21).
